items.prompt: What is the primary goal of weak supervision?
choices: Train using noisy, incomplete, or heuristic signals instead of only full hand labels
Require every training row to have expert gold labels
Eliminate all need for validation or gold checks
Replace storage systems with labeling platforms
items.explain: Section 11.5 defines weak supervision as using imperfect signals to reduce full hand labeling.

items.prompt: Heuristic labels always match true ground truth and need no auditing.
items.explain: Heuristic and distant labels are informative but imperfect and must be managed for noise.

items.prompt: Which is a common source of weak supervision?
choices: Domain heuristic rules or distant databases
Perfect human labels for every item only
Random coin flips with no domain signal
items.explain: Heuristics and distant supervision are central sources discussed in Section 11.5.

items.prompt: Name two advantages of weak supervision.
items.rubric: Cost-effectiveness, scalability/speed, or coverage when gold labels are scarce.
items.sample_answer: Lower labeling cost and ability to scale labels when clean gold sets are scarce.

items.prompt: Incomplete labeling means only a subset of the data has labels.
items.explain: Section 11.5 lists incomplete labels as a key weak-supervision characteristic.
